paragraphs.text:
## What SRD-46 shows

### 1) Amino acids with pKa data are abundant, but Zn²⁺ stability data cover only a subset
From the Zn²⁺ stability search over amino-acid-class ligands, SRD-46 contains Zn data for many amino-acid-like ligands, including simple α-amino acids and some side-chain-functionalized analogs. Among clearly standard amino acids in the returned Zn set, examples include glycine, norvaline/norleucine analogs, β-alanine, penicillamine analogs, and aromatic hydroxy analogs.

paragraphs.text:
### 2) The Zn-studied set establishes a donor-strength trend
The catalog/search results already indicate the trend:

| Ligand type in Zn data | Representative SRD-46 examples | Zn-binding implication |
|---|---|---|
| Simple amino/carboxylate | glycine, β-alanine, aminobutanoic acids | Baseline, moderate Zn binding |
| Extra N donor | imidazole-containing amino-acid-like ligands | Stronger than simple amino acids |
| Soft S donor | penicillamine, mercapto/thiaproline-like ligands | Strongest Zn binding among amino-acid-like ligands |
| Extra hard anionic O donor | aminomalonic / hydroxy-dicarboxylate-type amino acids | Should strengthen Zn binding relative to glycine-like ligands |

That trend is chemically sensible for Zn²⁺: extra donor atoms, especially **anionic carboxylate O** or strong N/S donors, improve stability.

claims.text: ### 2) The Zn-studied set establishes a donor-strength trend

| Simple amino/carboxylate | glycine, β-alanine, aminobutanoic acids | Baseline, moderate Zn binding |
| Extra N donor | imidazole-containing amino-acid-like ligands | Stronger than simple amino acids |
| Soft S donor | penicillamine, mercapto/thiaproline-like ligands | Strongest Zn binding among amino-acid-like ligands |
| Extra hard anionic O donor | aminomalonic / hydroxy-dicarboxylate-type amino acids | Should strengthen Zn binding relative to glycine-like ligands |
That trend is chemically sensible for Zn²⁺:
extra donor atoms, especially **anionic carboxylate O** or strong N/S donors, improve stability.

paragraphs.text:
## Why aspartic acid is the best unstudied standard amino acid

Among the standard amino acids not seen in the Zn results you retrieved, **aspartic acid** stands out because its acid–base pattern should make it a particularly good Zn ligand in water.

| Feature | Aspartic acid | Relevance to Zn²⁺ |
|---|---|---|
| α-amino group | Yes | N donor |
| α-carboxylate | Yes | Strong anionic O donor |
| Side-chain carboxylate | Yes | Second anionic O donor |
| Expected deprotonation behavior from amino-acid pKa logic | Two acidic groups can be deprotonated before the amino group | Gives an anionic, multidentate donor set favorable for Zn²⁺ |
| Chelation | Likely O,N and possibly O,O/N-assisted modes | Higher stability than simple mono-carboxylate amino acids |

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: Filler span: no useful database-groundable information.
General coordination chemistry: α-amino-dicarboxylates provide anionic/O,N donor sets favorable for Zn(II) aqueous binding; not a database row.
Aspartic acid structure includes an α-amino group (N donor). This is standard amino-acid chemistry; not given in SRD-46 rows here.
Aspartic acid has an α-carboxylate that deprotonates to an anionic O donor; established structure/property knowledge, not in tool rows.
Aspartic acid bears a side-chain carboxylate (–CH2–COOH), giving a second anionic O donor upon deprotonation; standard chemistry.
Typical pKa order: two COOH groups deprotonate well below the ammonium pKa, yielding an anionic, multidentate donor set; not a SRD-46 row.
O,N and O,O chelation by α-amino-dicarboxylates is a common motif; such chelation tends to enhance stability vs mono-carboxylate amino acids.

paragraphs.text:
## Why not methionine, lysine, serine, etc.?

| Candidate class | Why it likely ranks below Asp |
|---|---|
| Methionine | Thioether S is neutral and usually weaker than thiolate S; planner note explicitly warns not to over-rank thioethers |
| Lysine / arginine | Side-chain basic groups are strongly protonated in water, reducing effective donor availability |
| Serine / threonine | Alcohol O donors are weak unless deprotonated |
| Phenylalanine / leucine / valine / isoleucine | No extra donor group beyond backbone NH₂/COO⁻ |
| Glutamic acid | Also strong candidate, but typically a longer reach; the planner’s chemistry note favors **aspartate over glutamate** because a shorter chelate is often tighter |

claims.text: ## Why not methionine, lysine, serine, etc.?

| Lysine / arginine | Side-chain basic groups are strongly protonated in water, reducing effective donor availability |
| Serine / threonine | Alcohol O donors are weak unless deprotonated |
| Phenylalanine / leucine / valine / isoleucine | No extra donor group beyond backbone NH₂/COO⁻ |
| Glutamic acid | Also strong candidate, but typically a longer reach; the planner’s chemistry note favors **aspartate over glutamate** because a shorter chelate is often tighter |

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: Filler span: no useful database-groundable information.
General coordination chemistry: neutral thioether S donors bind weaker than anionic thiolate S; not explicitly encoded in SRD-46 rows here.
Established acid–base/coordination chemistry: Lys/Arg side chains are protonated near neutral pH, limiting donor availability; not directly stated in tool rows.
General chemistry: alcohol O is a weak donor unless deprotonated to alkoxide; not given as a database row in this run.
Amino acid structures: Phe/Leu/Val/Ile side chains lack extra donor atoms beyond the backbone NH2/COO−; not a database-stated fact here.
Chelation principle: shorter chelate rings (Asp) often bind tighter than longer (Glu). Not explicitly shown in the retrieved SRD-46 rows.

paragraphs.text:
## Best prediction

| Predicted unstudied amino acid | Reason |
|---|---|
| **Aspartic acid** | Adds a second carboxylate donor to the amino-acid backbone, giving the most favorable O/N donor set among likely unstudied standard amino acids for aqueous Zn²⁺ binding |

paragraphs.text:
## Confidence / nuance

| Point | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| If sulfur amino acids with **thiolate** donors were unstudied | They could outrank Asp, but cysteine-like strong S donors are already represented in the Zn dataset |
| Between Asp and Glu | Both should be strong, but **Asp** is the better pick because its shorter side chain often supports tighter chelation |
| Relative to histidine/cysteine | Asp is predicted as the best **unstudied** standard amino acid, not necessarily stronger than all studied amino acids already in SRD-46 |

So, **based on the available pKa/stability patterns in SRD-46, aspartic acid is the most defensible prediction for the strongest Zn²⁺ complex among standard amino acids not yet studied there**.

*Source: NIST SRD-46 searches on amino-acid pKa coverage and Zn²⁺ stability data.*
